food_subgroup: Sweet dessert pizza with chocolate-hazelnut spread and cream cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002682). No specific dessert pizza or Nutella pizza subclass exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pizza as a category. No dessert pizza or Nutella pizza subdivision exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for dessert pizza or Nutella pizza found in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is a whole individual dessert pizza of approximately 300 g, as served at Ricciotti restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Pizza dough (SR Legacy #18413), Nutella hazelnut spread (Branded #359168), Mascarpone cheese (SR Legacy #01254), Hazelnuts (SR Legacy #12120)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Branded Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Pizza Nutella exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. Nutrient values estimated from weighted combination of component ingredients: pizza dough base (~55% by weight), Nutella spread (~25%), mascarpone cream cheese (~15%), and crushed hazelnuts (~5%). Proportions based on standard restaurant dessert pizza preparation. Energy and macros calculated using Atwater factors cross-checked against USDA component values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of pizza and refined wheat-based baked goods with high-sugar toppings — analogous estimate based on pizza dough GI (GI=80) and Nutella/sugar-fat topping effect reducing overall GI to medium range; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Pizza Nutella exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Plain pizza dough has a published GI of approximately 80 (high). The addition of substantial fat (Nutella, mascarpone, hazelnuts) is well-documented to lower the glycemic response of wheat-based foods by slowing gastric emptying and glucose absorption. A blended GI of approximately 57 (medium range) is estimated using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method: pizza dough contributes ~55% of carbohydrates at GI 80, Nutella contributes ~40% of carbohydrates at GI ~55 (hazelnut-chocolate spread, analogous to chocolate spread GI per Sydney GI Database), and remaining ingredients contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (57 × 127.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 72.7, rounded to 73.1. This is a high glycemic load per serving due to large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~185 mg/100 g) and high caloric density; a full serving delivers approximately 555 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Pizza Nutella to occasional consumption and be mindful that a full 300 g serving contributes approximately 555 mg sodium (roughly 25% of the 2,000 mg daily limit), alongside high caloric load that may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~22 g/100 g), high glycemic load per serving (~73), and large refined carbohydrate load from pizza dough
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Pizza Nutella due to its very high glycemic load per serving (approximately 73), driven by the combination of refined wheat dough and high-sugar Nutella spread, which is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g), high sugar (~22 g/100 g), and high energy density (348 kcal/100 g) from Nutella and mascarpone
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Pizza Nutella as an occasional indulgence only, as a full serving provides approximately 20 g of saturated fat and 66 g of sugar, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pizza Nutella is an Italian dessert pizza originating from Italy and served in Italian restaurants worldwide, including in Singapore at establishments such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk. It is made from a standard leavened pizza dough base that is baked until golden and crisp, then generously spread with Nutella (a hazelnut-chocolate spread), topped with mascarpone cream cheese, and finished with crushed or whole hazelnuts. The result is a rich, indulgent dessert combining the chewy-crisp texture of pizza dough with the sweet, creamy, and nutty flavours of the toppings. Nutritionally, Pizza Nutella is energy-dense at approximately 348 kcal per 100 g, with a high sugar content (~22 g/100 g) and moderate-to-high saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g) from the mascarpone and Nutella. A full restaurant serving of approximately 300 g delivers around 1,044 kcal, 66 g of sugar, and a glycemic load of approximately 73, making it a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan, contains gluten, dairy, tree nuts (hazelnuts), and soy (from Nutella), and is not suitable for those with these allergens. It is typically consumed as a dessert or sweet snack and is not a nutritionally balanced meal component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Pizza Nutella?
What are the ingredients in Pizza Nutella?
What is the glycemic index of Pizza Nutella?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Pizza Nutella exists in any approved primary or secondary source. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredient data using weighted proportions based on standard restaurant dessert pizza preparation. GI is an analogous estimate using the carbohydrate-weighted method with published GI values for pizza dough and chocolate-hazelnut spread from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods (SR Legacy and Branded Foods) as no direct database entry for Pizza Nutella exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component source data for this food combination. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component source data for this food combination. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry moderate uncertainty. 5. GI of 57 (medium) is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pizza Nutella exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is listed as false because Nutella's halal certification varies by market and cannot be confirmed for the Singapore restaurant context without verification. 7. This food is Italian cuisine; multilingual naming rules for Italian cuisine do not mandate non-English local names under Project Veritas v2 (mandatory local-language rules apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes). Local names fields are null accordingly. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record appears to be a data error from the field survey platform and has been disregarded; Pizza Nutella contains no chicken.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input seed record. Estimated nutrients from USDA FoodData Central component data. Estimated GI using carbohydrate-weighted method with University of Sydney GI Database reference values. Populated all mandatory sections.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list — all entries confirmed present verbatim. Verified GI category threshold: GI=57 is in range 56-69, correctly classified as 'medium'. Verified -1 sentinel values documented in notes (vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg). Verified zero-macro justification not needed as no core macro is zero. Verified ontology match_type values are all within allowed set (exact, closest_parent, none). Confirmed G6 compliance: Italian cuisine has no mandatory non-English local name requirement. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass. Decision: PASS.
